questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with a 2-month history of generalised pruritus and fatigue. He has noticed intermittent jaundice. He drinks minimal alcohol and takes no regular medications. Examination reveals mild scleral icterus, no stigmata of chronic liver disease. LFTs: bilirubin 35 µmol/L, ALP 320 U/L, GGT 280 U/L, ALT 85 U/L, AST 70 U/L. Anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA) negative, ANA negative, smooth muscle antibody negative. Right upper quadrant ultrasound shows a normal gallbladder, normal-calibre common bile duct, and no intrahepatic duct dilatation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Secondary sclerosing cholangitis due to choledocholithiasis
questions.explanation: Cholestatic LFTs with pruritus and AMA negativity point away from PBC. Normal ultrasound makes secondary causes unlikely. The absence of smooth muscle antibody and predominant cholestatic pattern favours PSC over autoimmune hepatitis.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with ulcerative colitis for 5 years, currently in remission on mesalazine, is noted to have a raised ALP (220 U/L) on routine bloods. She is asymptomatic. Bilirubin and ALT are normal. Abdominal ultrasound shows a normal liver and bile ducts. Serology: AMA negative, p-ANCA positive, IgG4 normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The association with ulcerative colitis and p-ANCA positivity is classic for PSC. PBC is AMA-positive and not linked to IBD. Normal ultrasound and asymptomatic state make gallstones and fatty liver less likely.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with no prior medical history presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of right upper quadrant pain, rigors, and jaundice. Temperature 39.2°C, heart rate 105 bpm. There is right upper quadrant tenderness but no guarding. LFTs: bilirubin 85 µmol/L, ALP 450 U/L, ALT 120 U/L. Blood cultures grow Escherichia coli. Ultrasound shows dilated intrahepatic ducts, normal common bile duct, and no gallstones. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Acute cholangitis in a young man without stones or prior symptoms is a classic first presentation of PSC. Choledocholithiasis would show a stone on ultrasound. Cholecystitis usually has gallbladder tenderness and not necessarily duct dilatation. Cholangiocarcinoma typically presents more insidiously.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known primary sclerosing cholangitis for 10 years, maintained on ursodeoxycholic acid, presents with a 3-month history of progressive jaundice, pruritus, and unintentional weight loss of 8 kg. ERCP reveals a dominant stricture in the mid common bile duct. Brush cytology is inconclusive. Serum CA 19-9 is 520 U/mL (normal <37). What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Benign dominant stricture due to PSC
questions.explanation: In a PSC patient, a dominant stricture with weight loss and markedly elevated CA 19-9 is highly suspicious for cholangiocarcinoma, even with negative brushings. Benign stricture would not cause such systemic symptoms.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with fatigue and pruritus for 3 months. She has no past medical history, no diarrhoea, and no family history of liver disease. LFTs: ALP 280 U/L, GGT 210 U/L, ALT 70 U/L, bilirubin 18 µmol/L. AMA negative, p-ANCA positive, IgG4 normal. MRCP shows multifocal intrahepatic duct strictures with beading. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: MRCP findings of beading are diagnostic of PSC, even in the absence of IBD which is not always present. PBC is AMA-positive. IgG4 disease would have elevated IgG4 and often other organ involvement. Drug history is negative.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of pruritus and fatigue. He has no jaundice. Liver function tests show ALP 320 U/L (normal <130), ALT 65 U/L, and normal bilirubin. Abdominal ultrasound reveals a normal liver and bile ducts. What is the most appropriate next investigation to establish the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: MRCP is the non-invasive imaging modality of choice for diagnosing primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) due to its high sensitivity in detecting characteristic cholangiographic abnormalities such as multifocal strictures and beading. ERCP is reserved for therapeutic intervention, liver biopsy is not first-line, anti-mitochondrial antibody is for primary biliary cholangitis, and endoscopic ultrasound is not standard for PSC diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known ulcerative colitis (in remission) is noted to have a persistently elevated ALP of 280 U/L on routine blood tests. She is asymptomatic. What investigation is most likely to confirm the diagnosis of primary sclerosing cholangitis?
questions.explanation: MRCP is the first-line diagnostic test for PSC, particularly in patients with IBD and cholestatic liver biochemistry. It is non-invasive and avoids the risks of ERCP. Liver biopsy is reserved for cases where MRCP is equivocal. Anti-smooth muscle antibody is associated with autoimmune hepatitis, and raised IgG4 suggests IgG4-related disease.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known primary sclerosing cholangitis presents with fever, right upper quadrant pain, and jaundice. ALP is 600 U/L and bilirubin 150 µmol/L. Abdominal ultrasound shows dilated intrahepatic ducts. What investigation should be performed urgently to identify the cause of this deterioration?
questions.explanation: In acute cholangitis complicating PSC, urgent ERCP is indicated to diagnose and treat a dominant stricture or choledocholithiasis, as it allows both visualisation and therapeutic intervention. MRCP is non-invasive but delays definitive management. Blood cultures and CT are supportive but do not directly identify the biliary cause.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with known primary sclerosing cholangitis develops worsening pruritus and rising ALP. MRCP reveals a single tight stricture in the common bile duct. What is the single best investigation to differentiate a benign stricture from cholangiocarcinoma?
options: Endoscopic brushings for cytology during ERCP
questions.explanation: Endoscopic brush cytology obtained during ERCP is the standard method to detect malignancy in a dominant stricture, with specificity exceeding 90%. CT and CA19-9 have limited accuracy, PET-CT is not routinely recommended, and liver biopsy carries a high miss rate.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy presents with pruritus and jaundice for 2 weeks. LFTs show a cholestatic pattern (ALP 350 U/L, ALT 60 U/L, bilirubin 100 µmol/L). Abdominal ultrasound shows normal bile ducts and no gallstones. What is the most appropriate next test to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: MRCP is the first-line diagnostic test for PSC in children, as it is non-invasive and highly sensitive for detecting cholangiographic changes. ERCP is reserved for therapeutic indications. Anti-mitochondrial antibody is for primary biliary cholangitis (rare in this age), and sweat chloride is for cystic fibrosis (which can cause secondary sclerosing cholangitis but is not first-line).

questions.stem: A 28-year-old female is found to have raised alkaline phosphatase and gamma-glutamyl transferase. MRCP shows multiple strictures and saccular dilatations of the intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts. Liver biopsy confirms fibrous obliterative cholangitis. She is asymptomatic with normal bilirubin. What is the most appropriate first-line medical therapy to improve liver biochemistry?
questions.explanation: Ursodeoxycholic acid improves serum liver biochemistries in PSC and is recommended by NICE. Prednisolone and azathioprine are used for overlap with autoimmune hepatitis but not in isolated cholestatic PSC.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with known primary sclerosing cholangitis presents with moderate pruritus that disturbs his sleep. He has no jaundice and his liver enzymes are stable on ursodeoxycholic acid. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for his pruritus?
questions.explanation: Cholestyramine is a bile acid sequestrant and first-line for pruritus in cholestatic liver disease. Rifampicin, naltrexone and sertraline are second- or third-line options.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old with primary sclerosing cholangitis presents with worsening jaundice and a single episode of cholangitis. MRCP shows a dominant stricture in the common bile duct. What is the most appropriate first-line endoscopic management?
questions.explanation: Endoscopic balloon dilatation is the first-line treatment for dominant strictures in PSC. Stent insertion is associated with higher risk of cholangitis and occlusion and is reserved for refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with primary sclerosing cholangitis and a history of ulcerative colitis is found to have raised IgG, positive ANA and anti-smooth muscle antibodies, and liver histology shows interface hepatitis with bile duct damage. A diagnosis of PSC-autoinmune hepatitis overlap syndrome is made. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Overlap syndrome is treated with immunosuppression similar to autoimmune hepatitis, using prednisolone and azathioprine. Ursodeoxycholic acid alone would be insufficient for the hepatitis component.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with primary sclerosing cholangitis and compensated cirrhosis presents with dysuria and a urine culture growing Escherichia coli. She has had recurrent UTIs. Which of the following antibiotics should be avoided given her underlying liver disease?
questions.explanation: Nitrofurantoin can cause acute and chronic cholestatic liver injury and should be avoided in patients with cholestatic liver disease such as PSC. Trimethoprim and ciprofloxacin are safe alternatives for UTIs in this setting.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with ulcerative colitis presents with fatigue and pruritus. He is not jaundiced. Blood tests show ALP 350 U/L (normal <130), ALT 60 U/L, bilirubin 15 µmol/L. Abdominal ultrasound is normal. What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the suspected diagnosis of primary sclerosing cholangitis?
questions.explanation: MRCP is the first-line non-invasive diagnostic test for PSC. ERCP is reserved for therapy or when MRCP is inconclusive. Liver biopsy is not required unless small-duct PSC is suspected or dominant stricture needs further evaluation.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with known primary sclerosing cholangitis presents to the ED with rigors, right upper quadrant pain, fever of 39.2°C, and jaundice. Observations: HR 110 bpm, BP 90/60 mmHg, SpO2 96% on air. Bloods: WCC 18×10^9/L, CRP 240 mg/L, bilirubin 85 µmol/L, ALP 650 U/L. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start intravenous fluids and arrange MRCP
Take blood cultures and start intravenous antibiotics
Request an urgent CT abdomen with contrast
questions.explanation: This is acute cholangitis with sepsis. Immediate steps are cultures and broad-spectrum antibiotics per local guidelines, plus resuscitation. ERCP is indicated but not before antibiotics and stabilisation. MRCP is not urgent in this setting.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with primary sclerosing cholangitis is asymptomatic and on no treatment. Routine bloods show ALP 220 U/L (previously 150), ALT 45 U/L, bilirubin 12 µmol/L. She has no pruritus or jaundice. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Repeat liver function tests in 3 months
Arrange MRCP to reassess biliary tree
questions.explanation: In asymptomatic PSC with mild LFT rise, monitoring with repeat LFTs is appropriate. UDCA is not proven to improve outcomes and is not routinely recommended. Transplant assessment is for end-stage disease. Steroids are ineffective.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with primary sclerosing cholangitis develops worsening jaundice and pruritus over 2 weeks. MRCP shows a dominant stricture in the common bile duct with proximal dilation. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Add ursodeoxycholic acid to current treatment
Perform ERCP with balloon dilation ± temporary stent
Arrange CT-guided biopsy of the stricture
questions.explanation: Dominant strictures in PSC are managed endoscopically with dilation and/or stenting to relieve obstruction. Biopsy or surgery is reserved if features of malignancy. Transplant is for end-stage disease, not an isolated dominant stricture.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with primary sclerosing cholangitis and ulcerative colitis is in clinical remission on mesalazine. She has no biliary symptoms, and liver function tests are normal. What is the most appropriate next step for PSC surveillance?
options: Six-monthly colonoscopy for colorectal cancer surveillance
Annual liver function tests and abdominal ultrasound
questions.explanation: For PSC, annual LFTs and imaging (ultrasound or MRCP) are recommended to monitor disease progression and screen for cholangiocarcinoma. Colonoscopy is for UC surveillance, not specifically PSC. UDCA is not routinely recommended. ERCP is not for surveillance.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with known PSC and ulcerative colitis presents with a 1-day history of fever, rigors, and right upper quadrant pain. She is jaundiced. Which of the following clinical findings is most indicative of acute cholangitis requiring urgent biliary intervention?
questions.explanation: Rigors (severe shivering with fever) are a hallmark of acute cholangitis, indicating systemic infection. In the setting of PSC, this warrants urgent ERCP to relieve obstruction and prevent sepsis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with PSC and cirrhosis attends clinic with worsening fatigue and abdominal distension. His weight has increased by 5 kg over 2 weeks. On examination, his abdomen is tense with shifting dullness. Which of the following findings most clearly marks progression to decompensated cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Ascites is a key sign of decompensated cirrhosis, indicating portal hypertension and liver failure. It marks a significant clinical deterioration requiring management and consideration for transplant evaluation.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with PSC for 12 years presents with rapidly progressive jaundice, pruritus, and a 3 kg weight loss over 2 months. MRCP shows a new dominant stricture in the common bile duct. Which of the following findings would most strongly raise suspicion for cholangiocarcinoma?
questions.explanation: Positive brush cytology at ERCP is the gold standard for diagnosing cholangiocarcinoma. While elevated CA 19-9 can be suggestive, it is non-specific; a dominant stricture in PSC requires tissue sampling.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with known PSC and cirrhosis presents to A&E with haematemesis and melaena. He is tachycardic and hypotensive. Which of the following findings is most indicative of life-threatening variceal haemorrhage?
questions.explanation: Active bleeding seen on OGD confirms variceal haemorrhage and mandates immediate endoscopic therapy. Other findings are associated but not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman with PSC and cirrhosis is brought in by her husband with confusion and disorientation. She has asterixis. Which of the following clinical findings is most indicative of hepatic encephalopathy as the cause?
questions.explanation: Asterixis is a classic and specific sign of hepatic encephalopathy. In a patient with cirrhosis, its presence strongly supports the diagnosis and indicates acute decompensation requiring urgent assessment.
